titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Land Forces Adriatic Signals, Morale Report, Feb 45

div.note: LAND FORCES ADRIATIC SIGNALS.
MORALE REPORT
SECTION I.
Morale in general is good and reasonably high, particularly in view of the fighting on German soil. Morale is higher in sections engaged on operational work than it is in sections merely training.
SECTION II.
Factors affecting Morale. hhh 1. Discipline:

Unit and Section discipline is good and is reflected, in the general bearing and dress of the men. Daily parades, frequent checks and. periods of weapon training and P.T. assist the maintenance of a good standard of discipline. hhh 2. Confidence:
(a) In Army Leaders and the War. High, especially in view of the recent news.
(b) In Arms and Equipment. Great confidence is expressed in our fighting and technical equipment, although comment is sometimes made about the more generous scales held by the Americans.
(c) In Efficiency: All men are extremely confident of their efficiency and it is accepted as a routine factor in the composition of the British Army. hhh 3. Mail
Very satisfactory, since the de-rationing of A.M.L.C's and speeding up of mail from home. A few individual complaints, mainly due to people at home not writing. hhh 4. Welfare and Entertainment.
(a) Living conditions: As comfortable as circumstances permit. The men realise this.

(b) Recreation and Amusement. Inter-Section games (football) are played and recreational facilities are considered adequate. The distance of the Unit from BARI in relation to the cinemas and E.N.S.A. is a source of some complaint, despite Unit transport arrangements.
(c) General. Unit dances are held weekly and are very popular. Some grumble at recent repeated issue of dehydrated foods for meals. hhh 5. Education and A.B.C.A.

A few people have applied for Army Correspondence Courses. A.B.C.A. is quite popular and. gives rise to lively debates. A rather cynical view is taken about the Post-hostilities Education Scheme, which can be dispelled only when further information is released. hh 6. Relation between officers and men.
Good. A cordial and co-operative spirit is displayed between all ranks. hhh 7. Relations with other services.
Good, although there is not a great deal of contact, Signals normally working as independent detachments. hhh 8. Relations with Allies.

Good. A naive over-enthusiasm for the Russians, who have not been met in the flesh. A certain envy and prejudice of the Americans, because of their higher rate of pay and 'foreign' ways. Jugo-slavs now extremely unpopular. hhh 9. Home Affairs, Post-War and Release.

Government plans on post-war housing and general future regarded with some concern as to whether all these plans can be realised. Opinions differ widely. Release plan now generally accepted. hhh 10. Finance.
Hardly any financial worries. hhh 11. Miscellaneous.
(a) Overseas Service: Considered too long by all ranks.
(b) Leave: Most men want to go to ROME. LIAP is regarded as being a chance for the 'lucky' individual.
(c) Relations with Civil Population: Few contacts. A slightly contemptuous or condescending attitude blended with tolerance.
Field.
Feb, 45.
Major, R. Signals,
Comd Land Forces Adriatic Signals.
